facts: Martin Luther posted the 95 Theses in 1517 to challenge the Catholic Church's sale of indulgences, not to start a new denomination.
The term 'Protestant' originally referred to German princes who protested the Catholic Church's decision at the Diet of Speyer in 1529, not to Luther himself.
Lutheran, Calvinist, and Anglican are three distinct Protestant denominations that each defined their own beliefs after breaking from the Roman Catholic Church.
The Thirty Years' War (1618–1648) was a series of religious conflicts between Protestant and Catholic states that devastated central Europe and reshaped its political boundaries.
The Counter-Reformation was the Catholic Church's internal reform movement to address corruption and clarify doctrine, including the Council of Trent (1545–1563).
Martin Luther's 95 Theses were written in Latin for academic debate but were translated into German and spread widely by the printing press, sparking public debate.
misconceptions: Martin Luther wanted to leave the Catholic Church and start a new Protestant faith.
All Protestants share the same beliefs because they all broke away from the Catholic Church at the same time.
The Catholic Church made no changes in response to the Reformation.
